_meta.title: Topic Frameworks ↔ SA1 Chapter Map
_meta.description: Maps each SA1 chapter to the Topic_Frameworks.json keys that are most relevant to its content. Used by the SA1 frontend to pull cross-cutting topic content alongside chapter-specific revision notes. Topic keys are exact keys from Topic_Frameworks.json.

schema.sa1_chapters[].sa1_chapter: Chapter number as string key in SA1_Revision_Notes.json
schema.sa1_chapters[].topic_refs[].note: How the topic relates to this chapter
schema.sa1_chapters[].section_topic_map: Optional finer-grain: maps SA1 section titles to topic keys

rel_types.primary: This topic is a direct match — the chapter is largely about this topic
rel_types.supporting: Topic provides essential supporting content for this chapter
rel_types.contextual: Topic provides useful context or is partially covered in passing

integration_notes.renderer_hook: The SA1 renderer already has a SECTION_TOPIC_MAP constant (used by Framework view). This file provides the chapter-level equivalent, enabling a 'Related Topics' panel or inline topic-card injection per chapter.
integration_notes.deduplication: Where a topic appears under multiple chapters, the renderer should deduplicate if showing all topics for a session.
integration_notes.topic_card_display: Suggested display: in each chapter view, show a 'Related Frameworks' sidebar or collapsible panel listing topic cards from TOPIC_FRAMEWORKS keyed by this map.
integration_notes.six_pillar_overlay: The Six Pillar Framework (P1-P6) topic is a meta-topic that overlays all chapters — it should be surfaced as a persistent navigation element rather than tied to individual chapters.

topic_refs.note: The Six Pillar Framework is the structural overview introduced in Ch1 — the ACC and product cycle map directly to the six pillars.
High-level regulatory context (IRDAI overview) typically introduced in Ch1.
IP policy structure, benefit design, definitions — Ch2 covers the SA1 application of SP1 Ch1 material.
CI product design, condition definitions, tiered benefits — SA1 Ch2 extends SP1 Ch2.
LTCI pre-funded vs immediate needs, ADL triggers, benefit structures — SA1 Ch2 covers India-specific LTC context.
Disability definitions (own occ, ADL, FAT, PCA) are core to IP and LTCI products in Ch2.
Standalone vs rider design, unit-linked variants, options and guarantees — relevant to long-term products.
PMI product mechanics, scope, coverage, NCD, underwriting — core short-term product.
Health cash plan structure, community rating, benefit schedule — directly covered in Ch3.
MME fixed-benefit surgical schedule — covered in Ch3 alongside PMI.
ASU / short-term sickness products — closely linked to IP, covered in Ch3.
Group PMI, group IP variants — group product structures underpin short-term product distribution.
Overseas PMI and expatriate products are short-term PMI variants.
Product design framework — target market, benefit design, exclusions, options — maps directly to Ch4 product analysis.
Protection-specific design features (riders, unit-linked, GIOs) inform Ch4 product analysis.
Admin and IT constraints, TPA management, outsourcing — directly relevant to product analysis.
Customer needs lifecycle, gap analysis vs state provision — part of Ch4 product analysis framework.
State provision objectives, co-existence, demographic trends — directly maps to Indian public healthcare system.
Regulatory regime, IRDAI overview — propensity to buy is shaped by the regulatory environment.
Employer role in health provision — group IP, CI and PMI structures underpin the employer benefits discussion.
Economic and tax incentives influencing the business environment.
IRDAI regulatory regime, deeper dive — SA1 Ch6 extends the regulatory environment framework.
Specific IRDAI 2024 regulations — health insurance, products, PPH, EOM regulations.
Indian tax treatment of health premiums (Section 80D), GST impact — directly covered in Ch6.
Government-funded schemes (Ayushman Bharat), private-public interaction.
Modelling approaches — deterministic vs stochastic, multi-state, sensitivity — the foundation for product design decisions.
Demographic and financial assumptions — Ch7 builds on assumption-setting framework.
Pricing methodology — risk premium derivation, formula vs cashflow approach — core Ch7 content.
Data sources and quality for pricing — directly relevant to Ch7 pricing process.
Product design framework and constraints feed into the pricing model.
From risk premium to office premium, profit criteria, marketability, competitiveness — Ch8 extends the pricing build-up.
Rating strategies, NCD, experience rating, reviewable vs guaranteed premiums — directly mapped.
Underwriting strategy and anti-selection controls interact with pricing decisions.
Cashflow modelling approach, model office — underpins Ch8 profitability testing.
New business volume, risk discount rate — Ch16 assumptions directly feed Ch8.
Indian tax treatment — Section 80D, GST, tax incentives for health insurance — core Ch9 content.
IRDAI EOM regulations include taxation and expense constraints.
Regulatory frameworks, IRDAI powers, intervention tools, conduct of business — core Ch10 content.
IRDAI 2024 specific regulations: PPH, health insurance, products, AA regulations.
Actuarial professional obligations under IRDAI — AA statutory duties, APS 33/34.
Solvency II Pillar 1 — BEL, Risk Margin, SCR, MCR — core Ch11 content.
Risk types and quantification underpin Solvency II capital calculations.
Reserving principles (BEL, risk margin) directly map to Solvency II requirements.
Solvency II Pillars 2 and 3 — ORSA, governance, disclosure — Ch12 extends Pillar 1.
Solvency II disclosure requirements, SFCR, RSR — maps to Ch12 reporting content.
Risk governance and management framework under Solvency II Pillar 2.
Indian solvency regime (ASM/RSM), RBC transition, comparison with Solvency II — core Ch13 content.
IRDAI 2024 Actuarial, Finance & Investment regulations — solvency requirements specific to India.
IFRS 17 / Ind AS 117, RBC reporting requirements — Ch13 covers transition to new frameworks.
IFRS 17, Solvency II Pillar 3 disclosure, embedded value reporting — core Ch14 content.
Analysis of surplus — surplus sources, investigation framework — mapped directly to Ch14.
Reserve movements and valuation basis changes feed into profit reporting.
Actuarial professional duties, APS/TAS standards, ethics, whistleblowing — core Ch15 content.
Specific APS 33/34 and guidance notes — directly covered in Ch15.
IRDAI AA Regulations 2022, Appointed Actuary statutory obligations.
Capital optimisation, risk appetite, economic vs regulatory capital, reinsurance and ALM as capital tools.
Solvency ratio management, capital allocation, dividend policy — Ch16 extends solvency framework.
Reinsurance as a capital management tool — quota share, XL, financial reinsurance.
ALM and investment strategy as capital efficiency tools.
Reinsurance structures, retention levels, treaty vs facultative, financial reinsurance — core Ch17 content.
Reinsurance as a risk mitigation tool — connected to the broader risk management framework.
IRDAI reinsurance regulations 2024 — FRBs, CBRs, PMC approval requirements.
Investment strategy, ALM principles, asset characteristics, liability matching — core Ch18 content.
ALM as a risk management tool — investment and liquidity risks in the capital framework.
Investment strategy interacts with solvency capital requirements and free asset position.
Experience investigation framework — morbidity, persistency, expense, investment monitoring — Ch19 is the core application.
Data requirements by product type, data quality criteria — directly underpin experience analysis.
Assumption-setting process feeds from experience analysis results — the A/E feedback loop.

sa1_chapters.sa1_title: Analysis of Surplus, Embedded Value and Profit

topic_refs.note: Analysis of surplus sources, embedded value reconciliation — Ch20 covers both in depth.
EV reporting, investor reporting, IFRS 17 profit emergence — Ch20 connects monitoring to external reporting.
Reserve movements and valuation basis changes feed into surplus analysis.
Strategy assessment is the culmination of the Six Pillar Framework — Ch21 applies it to full strategic scenarios.
Risk framework and capital considerations are central to strategy assessment.
Regulatory constraints and IRDAI obligations shape strategic options.
Structuring and justifying strategy recommendations — the Six Pillar framework provides the scaffold.
Risk-adjusted strategy recommendations require capital and risk framing.
Regulatory compliance and IRDAI obligations must be embedded in strategy recommendations.
Risk taxonomy (17 categories) — second-order strategy implications map directly to risk categories.
Capital and risk implications of strategic decisions — operational, market, credit risk dimensions.
Stakeholder perspectives and ACC applied — tracing second-order implications across all pillars.
State provision objectives, co-existence models, funding approaches, international comparisons — Ch24 applies SP1 Ch10 in depth.
Regulatory interaction with national healthcare systems — IRDAI, PMJAY, government policy.
India-specific healthcare system context (Ayushman Bharat, ESIC, CGHS) — comparative market insight.
Underwriting best practice — anti-selection controls, policy review, automation governance.
Claims management best practice — triage, fraud detection, declined claims analysis.
Best practice is underpinned by actuarial professional standards and ethics.
Data quality management is a best practice dimension — experience monitoring feedback.
Solving complex exam-style questions requires applying the Six Pillar Framework as a structured approach.
Risk identification across the 17 categories is a core tool for tackling complex issues.
Glossary terms span all pillars — the Six Pillar framework provides a useful organisational lens.
